content.text:
Lagrange's Theorem is not merely a theoretical result; it is a powerful 
tool for solving problems. In this section, we explore several 
applications.

content.title: Groups of Order $2p$ Structure
content.content:
Let $G$ be a group of order $2p$ where $p$ is an odd prime. Then $G$ is 
either cyclic or isomorphic to $D_p$ (the dihedral group of order $2p$).
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Groups of Order $pq$
content.content:
Let $G$ be a group of order $pq$ where $p < q$ are primes and $p \nmid 
(q-1)$. Then $G$ is cyclic.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The full proof uses material from later chapters (Sylow theorems), but 
the key insight is that element orders are severely constrained.

content.title: Group of order $15$
content.problem:
Any group of order $15 = 3 \cdot 5$ must be cyclic since $3 \nmid (5-1) 
= 4$.

content.title: Elements of order $5$ in $\Z_{20}$
content.problem:
By Lagrange, elements of order $5$ can only exist in a group whose order 
is divisible by $5$. In $\Z_{20}$, $5 \mid 20$, so such elements can exist.

The subgroup of order $5$ is $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8, 12, 16\}$. 
The generators of this cyclic group have order $5$:
\[
 \{4, 8, 12, 16\}
\]
(all except $0$). There are $\phi(5) = 4$ elements of order $5$.

content.title: No subgroup of order $5$ in $S_4$
content.problem:
Since $|S_4| = 24$ and $5 \nmid 24$, Lagrange's Theorem implies that 
$S_4$ has no subgroup of order $5$.

content.title: Subgroup structure from divisibility
content.problem:
Let $|G| = 100 = 2^2 \cdot 5^2$. Possible subgroup orders are:
\[
 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100
\]
There can be no subgroup of order $3$, $6$, $7$, $8$, etc.

content.text:
RSA encryption relies on the difficulty of factoring large numbers. The 
decryption process uses Euler's theorem.

content.title: RSA principle
content.problem:
If $n = pq$ (product of distinct primes), then $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. 
For any $m$ with $\gcd(m, n) = 1$:
\[
 m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
\]
If $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, then:
\[
 (m^e)^d = m^{ed} = m^{1 + k\phi(n)} = m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv m \pmod{n}
\]

items: How many elements of order $7$ are in $\Z_{49}$?
Prove that any group of order $77 = 7 \times 11$ is cyclic.

Let $|G| = 24$. What are the possible orders of elements? Must 
 $G$ have an element of each possible order?

If $G$ has exactly one subgroup of order $d$ for each divisor 
 $d$ of $|G|$, prove that $G$ is cyclic.

marginNotes.text:
The beginning of wisdom is this: Get 
wisdom.
